elements.sent-2411-001: Recently, large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in abstractive summarization tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, controllable summarization with LLMs remains underexplored, limiting their ability to generate summaries that align with specific user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we first investigate the capability of LLMs to control diverse attributes, revealing that they encounter greater challenges with numerical attributes, such as length and extractiveness, compared to linguistic attributes.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this challenge, we propose a guide-to-explain framework (GTE) for controllable summarization.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our GTE framework enables the model to identify misaligned attributes in the initial draft and guides it in explaining errors in the previous output.
elements.sent-2411-006: Based on this reflection, the model generates a well-adjusted summary.
elements.sent-2411-007: As a result, by allowing the model to reflect on its misalignment, we generate summaries that satisfy the desired attributes in surprisingly fewer iterations than other iterative methods solely using LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-008: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated superior performance in abstractive summarization, outperforming traditional encoder-decoder models by generating more contextually appropriate and natural summaries \cite{goyal2023news, zhang2023benchmarking, pu2023summarization, ryu24_interspeech .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In addition, recent studies aimed to generate higher-quality summaries by leveraging the self-correction capabilities of LLMs \cite{zhang-etal-2023-summit, sun-etal-2024-prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, given individuals' diverse preferences for summary styles, it is essential to generate summaries that adjust personal needs \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum .}
elements.sent-2411-011: For instance, some users may prefer concise summaries or retain exact phrases from the original text.
elements.sent-2411-012: Therefore, controllable summarization has recently garnered attention \cite{zhong-etal-2021-qmsum, xu-etal-2023-lmgqs, zhang-etal-2023-macsum .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Previous research employed encoder-decoder models to control attributes \cite{mao-etal-2022-dyle, zhang-etal-2022-summn, vig-etal-2022-exploring,pagnoni-etal-2023-socratic, wang-etal-2023-instructive, urlana-etal-2024-controllable .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Although LLMs excel in generating high-quality summaries, they still face challenges in controlling attributes \cite{yuan2024followinglengthconstraintsinstructions, tang-etal-2023-context , and their controllability has been underexplored \cite{liu-etal-2024-benchmarking .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, we analyze LLMs' ability to control various attributes in summarization and refine the measurements to more accurately assess these attributes.
elements.sent-2411-016: We reveal that while LLMs excel at controlling linguistic attributes such as topic and speaker, they severely struggle with numerical attributes such as extractiveness and length.
elements.sent-2411-017: To address this challenge, we propose a guide-to-explain (GTE), which enables precise attribute control solely through LLMs without relying on external modules or training.
elements.sent-2411-018: We first design an attribute-identification step to calculate misaligned attributes in LLM-generated summaries, subsequently guiding the model to explain the sources of its errors.
elements.sent-2411-019: By self-reflecting its own errors, the model can adequately adjust attributes in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-020: We introduce the self-refine strategy, primarily used in reasoning tasks with LLMs \cite{weng-etal-2023-large, self-refine-2023, dhuliawala-etal-2024-chain, gou2024critic , to controllable summarization.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Additionally, we evaluate GTE on mixed attribute control datasets, MACSum$_{Doc}$ and MACSum$_{Dial}$ \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum .}
elements.sent-2411-022: GTE successfully controls each attribute with minimal iterations solely using LLMs, outperforming other iteration methods.
elements.sent-2411-023: We also demonstrate the high quality of the controlled summaries via multiple evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-024: In addition, we analyzed whether LLMs can control multiple attributes simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-025: We found out that LLMs struggle with jointly controlling correlated numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-027: We revisit the measurement of various attributes and analyze LLMs' controllability.
elements.sent-2411-028: We propose a guide-to-explain (GTE), which guides the model to explain its misalignments.
elements.sent-2411-029: GTE effectively adjusts misaligned attributes only in a few iterations.
elements.sent-2411-030: We investigate the controllability of LLMs for four attributes: extractiveness, length, topic, and speaker.
elements.sent-2411-031: Extractiveness evaluates how much of the summary's content directly overlaps with the original text.
elements.sent-2411-032: A highly extractive summary is required when users need to retain the original context, such as in academic papers; however, paraphrasing is applied to tailor the summary in general cases.
elements.sent-2411-033: The length counts the ratio between the main text and the summary.
elements.sent-2411-034: The preferred summary length varies depending on the information density of the text and user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-035: For topics or speakers, users may prefer summaries focused on a specific topic or speakers from a long document or dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-036: Previous methods have not effectively accounted for attribute-focused aspects.
elements.sent-2411-037: In the case of extractiveness, it is straightforward to determine how much of the summary's content directly overlaps with the original text.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, for length, prompts suggested by earlier works specify a fixed number of sentences, e.g., "3 sentences," but this approach fails to account for variations in sentence length and does not accurately reflect the summary's actual length \cite{goyal2023news,liu-etal-2024-benchmarking, yuan2024followinglengthconstraintsinstructions .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Thus, we calculate the summary length as a ratio relative to the main text.
elements.sent-2411-040: For topics, \citet{zhang-etal-2023-macsum calculated the frequency of topic-related words in the summary.}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, even if topic words do not explicitly appear, the summary can still reflect the core context of the topic, especially for LLM-generated summaries, which tend to paraphrase the content.
elements.sent-2411-042: Therefore, we compute the embedding similarity $\mathcal{B}$ between the topic word and each word in the summary $s$: $\frac{1 {n \sum_{i \in s}\mathcal{B (topic, word_{i )$, where $n$ is the number of words in the summary.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: If multiple topics $k$ are present, we use the average embedding similarity across all topics: $\frac{1 {k}\sum_{j \in k}\frac{1 {n \sum_{i \in s}\mathcal{B (topic_{k , word_{i )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: For speakers, \citet{zhang-etal-2023-macsum calculate the frequency of the speaker's spoken words appearing in the summary.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In contrast, we extract speaker utterances and construct speaker set $\mathcal{S}$ and utilize BERTScore \cite{zhang2020bertscore to compute the embedding similarity between the summary $p$ and $\mathcal{S}$: BERTScore$(p, \mathcal{S )$.}}

elements.sent-2411-046: We use the publicly available MACSum dataset \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum for controllable summarization.}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, existing labels for numerical attributes are ambiguous as criteria degrees are not specified (e.g., high for extractiveness and short for length).
elements.sent-2411-048: To provide detailed criteria, we reinterpret the labels based on the attribute distributions in each training set.
elements.sent-2411-049: For extractiveness, we set labels as \{normal: 85\%, high: 90\%, fully: 100\%\}.
elements.sent-2411-050: Unlike previous methods, we define the summary length as a ratio of the original text rather than a fixed value.
elements.sent-2411-051: Since the existing labels do not distinctly differentiate these ratios, we set the lengths to \{short: 7.5\%, normal: 15\%, long: 32.5\%\}, providing clearer distinctions with evaluating a broader range of controllability.
elements.sent-2411-052: Labeling details can be found in Appendix A.
elements.sent-2411-053: We evaluate the ability of LLMs to adjust their outputs based on specified attributes (Table \ref{tab: main_doc , \ref{tab: main_dial ).}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Our assessment includes two evaluations: (1) the failure rate upon reaching the predefined maximum iterations without achieving the desired modifications and (2) the average iterations required to adjust an attribute, calculated only for successful cases.
elements.sent-2411-055: We denote the naive iteration approach, which simply adjusts attributes repeatedly, as Iter.
elements.sent-2411-056: Most LLMs effectively control linguistic attributes, such as topic and speaker.
elements.sent-2411-057: However, LLMs struggle with numerical attributes, including extractiveness and length.
elements.sent-2411-058: Both Llama-70B and GPT-4o-Iter show a very high failure rate with numerous iterations.
elements.sent-2411-059: In particular, LLMs struggle to adjust for normal extractiveness and long length attributes.
elements.sent-2411-060: Therefore, we introduce a guide-to-explain (GTE) framework (Figure~\ref{fig: main ) to adjust the challenging numerical attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-061: We provide an attribute identification step to adjust incorrectly generated responses and guide the LLMs to reflect by explaining the reasons behind these errors.
elements.sent-2411-062: Our approach allows the model to make appropriate corrections in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-063: We first prompt the LLM to generate an initial draft $s'$ that reflects the specified attributes $i$.
elements.sent-2411-064: If the LLM fails to control the instructed attributes accurately, we provide attribute identification step (AIS) to guide the model on how to adjust the attributes.
elements.sent-2411-065: LLM may have difficulty measuring attributes such as extractiveness or the length ratio relative to the original text.
elements.sent-2411-066: Thus, we provide a step-by-step approach to instruct the model on revising its summary based on the generated output.
elements.sent-2411-067: After providing instructions AIS on revising the summary, we supply guidance step (GS) to the model to explain why it initially failed to adjust the attributes correctly.
elements.sent-2411-068: This process is similar to how humans solve complex problems by reviewing their mistakes to produce more accurate responses in the future.
elements.sent-2411-069: Receiving [$a$; $i$, $s'$; AIS; GS] as inputs, where $ a$ denotes the article, the model first reflects on the reasons for the initial error before generating a revised summary.
elements.sent-2411-070: If the revised summary still fails to satisfy the attributes, GTE repeats until the model produces an attribute-compliant summary.
elements.sent-2411-071: As LLMs are known to struggle with number-related tasks \cite{thawani-etal-2021-representing, imani-etal-2023-mathprompter , our guidance to explain why their calculation is incorrect, followed by generating summaries assists in effectively controlling the numeric attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The used prompts are in Appendix B.
elements.sent-2411-073: We evaluate the controllability of various LLMs, including Phi-3 \cite{abdin2024phi , Llama3 \cite{dubey2024llama , and GPT series \cite{brown2020language, achiam2023gpt .}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: To analyze model performance by size, we use both the 8B and quantized 70B versions \footnote{casperhansen/llama-3-70b-instruct-awq of Llama3, and GPT-3.5 and GPT-4.}
elements.sent-2411-075: We used two datasets, the MACSum$_{Doc}$ and the MACSum$_{Dial}$ datasets \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum , which comprise committee meetings and news contents each.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Both datasets are for mixed-attribute summarization that control multiple attributes simultaneously, but only MACSum$_{Dial}$ has speaker attribute.
elements.sent-2411-077: Since we evaluate LLM performance on individual attributes, we use attributes separately.
elements.sent-2411-078: We define the strategy solely providing attribute identification steps as AIS and denote our full guiding framework as GTE.
elements.sent-2411-079: As a result, our GTE demonstrates remarkably lower failure rates and fewer iterations when adjusting summaries across all attributes, including challenging numerical attributes in MACSum$_{Doc}$ (Table \ref{tab: main_doc ).}
elements.sent-2411-080: Notably, while applying GTE to smaller models such as Phi-3 and Llama3-8B resulted in significant performance improvements, we observed that failures were almost nonexistent when applied to larger models such as Llama-70B or GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-081: LLMs encounter more difficulties with the MACSum$_{Dial}$ dataset (Table \ref{tab: main_dial ).}
elements.sent-2411-082: The dataset, which is derived from QMSum \cite{zhong-etal-2021-qmsum , consists of lengthy and diverse content parliamentary and committee meetings, making it more challenging compared to the CNN-news-based MACSum$_{Doc}$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Notably, both the GPT-4o-Iter and GPT-4o-AIS failed to adjust for long length, whereas our GPT-4o-GTE demonstrated a commendable success rate.
elements.sent-2411-084: Regarding extractiveness, the Iter and AIS of GPT-4o exhibit relatively low iteration counts since the models mostly exceed the maximum iteration ($\circlearrowright$).
elements.sent-2411-085: While they fail nearly 80\%, our GTE demonstrates a significantly lower failure rate at 17.28\% with low iterations.
elements.figure-2411-004: 0.60{ \begin{tabular}{l|cccc|c} Model \multicolumn{1}{l}{Coherence} \multicolumn{1}{l}{Consistency} \multicolumn{1}{l}{Fluency} \multicolumn{1}{l|}{Relevance} QuestEval Iter (Ext) 0.820 0.800 0.859 0.696 0.523 AIS (Ext) 0.884 0.843 0.905 0.785 0.554 Iter (Len) 0.836 0.803 0.836 0.759 0.484 AIS (Len) 0.934 0.834 0.942 0.887 0.548 GTE (Ext) 0.941 0.873 0.937 0.880 0.590 GTE (Len) 0.937 0.840 0.944 0.901 0.553 \end{tabular} }\caption{Quality of the controlled summaries.}\label{tab: quality}
elements.sent-2411-086: Instead of ROUGE score\cite{lin-2004-rouge , which does not adequately evaluate the quality of the summaries \cite{zhong-etal-2022-towards, scialom-etal-2021-questeval, ryu-etal-2024-multi , we use UniEval \cite{zhong-etal-2022-towards and QuestEval \cite{scialom-etal-2021-questeval to assess both the inherent quality of the summaries and their factual consistency with the source text.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: UniEval, a multi-dimensional evaluator with high human correlation, assesses dimensions such as coherence, consistency, fluency, and relevance, and QuestEval evaluates factuality via question answering.
elements.sent-2411-088: Table \ref{tab: quality shows that our method's summaries outperform all UniEval dimensions and QuestEval scores, demonstrating effective attribute control while maintaining overall summary quality.}
elements.sent-2411-089: We observed that when generating summaries controlled for all attributes simultaneously, the model effectively handled linguistic attributes but faced challenges with numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-090: Notably, satisfying all attributes within the maximum number of iterations proved challenging for all methods, including GTE.
elements.sent-2411-091: Discovering the challenges in precisely controlling all attributes in parallel, we introduce a sequential-planning strategy, which gradually adjusts attributes from the ill-controlled with the initial draft using GTE.
elements.sent-2411-092: However, modifying one attribute often disrupted previously adjusted attributes due to correlations.
elements.sent-2411-093: For example, controlling length first would still lead to changes in length when adjusting extractiveness.
elements.sent-2411-094: Consequently, sequential adjustments result in a modest performance gap compared to the initial draft (Figure \ref{fig: mixed ).}
elements.sent-2411-095: We assess the attributes using the root mean squared error (RMSE) between the instructed values and those in the generated summaries.
elements.sent-2411-096: In this work, we revisit the measurement of controllable summarization with various attributes.
elements.sent-2411-097: We evaluate the controllability of multiple attributes in summary generation with LLMs, finding that LLMs struggle to adjust numeric attributes compared to linguistic ones.
elements.sent-2411-098: To address this limitation, we propose a guide-to-explain (GTE) approach, where the model is guided to explain its misalignments and then grounded this explanation to produce better-controlled summaries in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-099: GTE enables LLMs to control challenging numerical attributes with lower failure and fewer iterations.
elements.sent-2411-100: Further, we validate the quality of the controlled summaries via a multi-dimensional evaluation, demonstrating the high-quality generation.
elements.sent-2411-101: We evaluated the controllability of various attributes in LLMs and introduced a novel guide-to-explain (GTE) framework to address challenges in numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-102: While GTE enhanced control over numerical attributes, it still struggled with highly correlated mixed numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-103: Additionally, sequential planning, which adjusts attributes in order of least alignment, also faced difficulties achieving precise control.
elements.sent-2411-104: Even after properly adjusting one attribute, modifying the correlated numerical attribute caused the previously adjusted attribute to change.
elements.sent-2411-105: We believe further research could explore more effective methods for addressing these challenges.
elements.sent-2411-106: We used publicly available MACSum datasets for our research, conducting experiments with Phi-3, Llama3 \footnote{Meta Llama3 Community License, Copyright © Meta Platforms, Inc.. All Rights Reserved.}
elements.sent-2411-107: More details can be found at: \href{https://llama.meta.com/llama3/license/ {Llama3 License , GPT-3.5, and GPT-4o from April to October 2024.}}

elements.sent-2411-109: We analysis the data distributions in the MACSum$_{Doc}$ and MACSum$_{Dial}$ \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The attributes used in the original datasets are described as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-111: Extractiveness: Controls how much of the summary is directly extracted from the source text.
elements.sent-2411-112: It is evaluated using the average of ROUGE-2 and ROUGE-3 precision scores.
elements.sent-2411-113: Length: The number of words in the summary.
elements.sent-2411-114: It is evaluated based on token length.
elements.sent-2411-115: Topic: Control the summary to align with the given topic, and multiple topics can be present.
elements.sent-2411-116: The evaluation is based on the proportion of topic words appearing in the summary.
elements.sent-2411-117: Speaker: Control the summary to focus on the speech of a specific speaker.
elements.sent-2411-118: It is evaluated by the ratio of the speaker's words included in the summary.
elements.sent-2411-119: Specificity: Controls the level of detail or descriptive content in the summary.
elements.sent-2411-120: The evaluation formula is: $Specificity=(0.1 \times verbs + 0.2 \times tokens + 0.3 \times nouns + 0.4 \times numerical \text{ tokens) / number \text{ of \text{ sentences$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The detailed data distribution for both MACSum$_{Doc}$ and MACSum$_{Dial}$ are presented in Table \ref{tab: data_distribution .}
elements.sent-2411-122: We observe that the labels in previous work do not account for attributes.
elements.sent-2411-123: In the case of topic, the specific topic keywords may not always appear explicitly, even if the summary is focused on the given topic.
elements.sent-2411-124: Similarly, for speaker, LLMs may paraphrase the speaker's words using different terms or expressions.
elements.sent-2411-125: Therefore, we reinterpreted these labels in Section 2.1 to account for these factors.
elements.sent-2411-126: For the extractiveness and length attributes, the original labels were categorized as {‘normal’, ‘high’, ‘fully’ for extractiveness and {‘short’, ‘normal’, ‘long’ for length.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: However, since the labels were not numerically defined, we deemed the criteria unclear.
elements.sent-2411-128: Therefore, we redefined these labels for our use.
elements.sent-2411-129: We calculate extractiveness based on the proportion of words in the source text that appeared exactly in the summary.
elements.sent-2411-130: For length, while the original dataset was labelled based on word count and compression rate, we only considered the ratio of the summary to the original text.
elements.sent-2411-131: Table \ref{tab: data_distribution shows that the ratios were not strictly distinct, with some overlap between labels.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Thus, we relabeled boundaries in Section \ref{sec: controllable attributes .}
elements.sent-2411-133: Since these label values are not exact for the reference summary, we adjusted attributes according to the specified new labels.
elements.sent-2411-134: Consequently, we did not use the reference summaries from the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-135: For specificity, we used the Flair \footnote{https://github.com/flairNLP/flair \cite{akbik2019flair model for named entity recognition (NER) model to measure the number of entities in each summary.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: Since the number of entities varies with summary length, we calculated specificity as the ratio of total entities to total words in the summary.
elements.sent-2411-137: However, the annotation for specificity in the original dataset was inversely related to our calculations, so we did not use this attribute in our study.
elements.sent-2411-138: We set attribute-specific thresholds and iteratively adjusted them until the criteria were satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-139: For extractiveness and length, iterations were considered successful if the values fell within a relabeled value $\pm$5 range.
elements.sent-2411-140: For the topic and speaker, we set the minimum embedding similarity score as the criterion for achieving a successful topic- or speaker-focused summary.
elements.sent-2411-141: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in abstractive summarization tasks.
elements.sent-2411-142: However, their ability to precisely control summary attributes (e.g., length or topic) remains underexplored, limiting their adaptability to specific user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-143: In this paper, we systematically explore the controllability of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-144: To this end, we revisit summary attribute measurements and introduce iterative evaluation metrics, failure rate and average iteration count to precisely evaluate controllability of LLMs, rather than merely assessing errors.
elements.sent-2411-145: Our findings show that LLMs struggle more with numerical attributes than with linguistic attributes.
elements.sent-2411-146: Our GTE framework enables the model to identify misaligned attributes in the initial draft and guides it in self-explaining errors in the previous output.
elements.sent-2411-147: By allowing the model to reflect on its misalignment, GTE generates well-adjusted summaries that satisfy the desired attributes with robust effectiveness, requiring surprisingly fewer iterations than other iterative approaches.
elements.sent-2411-148: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated superior performance in text summarization, outperforming encoder-decoder models by generating more contextually appropriate and natural summaries \cite{goyal2023news, zhang2023benchmarking, pu2023summarization, ryu24_interspeech .}
elements.sent-2411-149: However, given individuals' diverse preferences for summary styles, it is essential to generate summaries tailored to individual needs \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum .}
elements.sent-2411-150: For instance, some users may prefer specific topic-focused summaries or retain exact phrases.
elements.sent-2411-151: While LLMs excel in generating fluent summaries, their ability to precisely control attributes remains underexplored \cite{liu-etal-2024-benchmarking , limiting their adaptability to diverse user preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Controllable summarization has recently garnered attention \cite{zhong-etal-2021-qmsum, ahuja-etal-2022-aspectnews, maddela-etal-2022-entsum, mehra-etal-2023-entsumv2, xu-etal-2023-lmgqs, zhang-etal-2023-macsum , with prior studies primarily utilizing encoder-decoder architectures \cite{mao-etal-2022-dyle, zhang-etal-2022-summn, vig-etal-2022-exploring, he-etal-2022-ctrlsum, pagnoni-etal-2023-socratic, wang-etal-2023-instructive, urlana-etal-2024-controllable that incorporate attribute-specific training signals to constrain summary generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: However, these systems lack scalability due to their dependence on attribute-specific training.
elements.sent-2411-154: In contrast, LLMs offer flexibility yet rely on in-context learning and prompting without explicit training-based constraints, hindering fine-grained attribute control \cite{yuan2024followinglengthconstraintsinstructions, tang-etal-2023-context , necessitating further investigation.}
elements.sent-2411-155: To systematically explore the controllability of LLMs, we first revisit the measurements for four key attributes: extractiveness, length, topic, and speaker, and refine them for more precise measurement.
elements.sent-2411-156: Specifically, instead of previous attribute measurement strategies that rely solely on word presence for topic- or speaker-focused summaries, we adopt embedding similarity to incorporate semantic information into the measurements.
elements.sent-2411-157: Building on this refined attribute measurement, we move beyond the previous controllability evaluation, which primarily assesses the disparity between the reference and the generated summary \cite{he-etal-2022-ctrlsum, zhang-etal-2023-macsum .}
elements.sent-2411-158: To fully explore the LLMs' controllability, we shift focus from merely measuring attribute errors to evaluating whether LLMs can accurately control specified attributes via iterative refinement.
elements.sent-2411-159: Thus, we introduce two evaluation metrics: the failure rate—the proportion of control failures within the maximum iterations—and the average iteration count required for successful control.
elements.sent-2411-160: Our findings reveal that while LLMs excel at controlling linguistic attributes such as topic and speaker, they severely struggle with numerical attributes such as extractiveness and length.
elements.sent-2411-161: We assume that unlike linguistic attributes, which rely on semantic coherence, numerical ones demand strict quantitative constraints, making fine-grained control challenging.
elements.sent-2411-162: To address this challenge, we propose a guide-to-explain (GTE) framework, which enables precise attribute control solely through LLMs without relying on additional attribute-specific training.
elements.sent-2411-163: We first design a step-by-step attribute identification phase to instruct the model on calculating misaligned attributes of its previously generated summary, then guide it to explain the rationales behind its errors.
elements.sent-2411-164: Through self-reflection, the model corrects its previous mistakes and generates a well-aligned summary in the regeneration phase.
elements.sent-2411-165: By integrating a self-refinement strategy—proven effective in complex reasoning tasks \cite{weng-etal-2023-large, self-refine-2023, dhuliawala-etal-2024-chain, gou2024critic —into controllable summarization, we enhance LLMs' attribute controllability while ensuring summary quality.}
elements.sent-2411-166: We evaluate GTE on mixed-attribute summarization datasets, MACSum$_{Doc}$ and MACSum$_{Dial}$ \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum .}
elements.sent-2411-167: GTE successfully controls each attribute with minimal iterations, significantly outperforming other iteration methods, while demonstrating robustness by consistently adjusting attributes across data samples.
elements.sent-2411-168: Further, we also demonstrate the high quality of the controlled summaries across multiple generic summarization evaluation metrics, including UniEval \cite{zhong-etal-2022-towards and QuestEval \cite{scialom-etal-2021-questeval .}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Finally, we analyze whether LLMs can control multiple attributes simultaneously, revealing their struggles in jointly managing correlated numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-170: We systematically explore LLM's controllability in text summarization.
elements.sent-2411-171: We refine the measurement of summarization attributes and introduce iterative evaluation metrics for evaluating LLMs' controllability.
elements.sent-2411-172: We propose a guide-to-explain (GTE), which guides the model to explain its misalignments and effectively adjusts misaligned attributes within just a few iterations.
elements.figure-2411-013: 0.68{ \begin{tabular}{lll} Attribute Metrics Paper Extractiveness ROUGE, word overlap \citet{goyal-etal-2022-hydrasum, zhang-etal-2023-macsum} Length Absolute length, length ratio \citet{goyal-etal-2022-hydrasum, he-etal-2022-ctrlsum, maddela-etal-2022-entsum, zhang-etal-2023-macsum} Topic, Query ROUGE, LDA, topic word count, classifier \citet{zhong-etal-2021-qmsum, he-etal-2022-ctrlsum, zhang-etal-2023-macsum, xu-etal-2023-lmgqs} Speaker, Entity ROUGE, speaker utterance word overlap \citet{maddela-etal-2022-entsum, zhang-etal-2023-macsum} \end{tabular} } \caption{Previous methods for measuring attributes.} \label{tab: evaluation_survey}
elements.sent-2411-173: Controllable summarization has recently gained attention due to its practical applications \cite{zhong-etal-2021-qmsum, ahuja-etal-2022-aspectnews, maddela-etal-2022-entsum, mehra-etal-2023-entsumv2, xu-etal-2023-lmgqs, zhang-etal-2023-macsum, ribeiro-etal-2023-generating .}
elements.sent-2411-174: Previous research employed encoder-decoder models to control attributes \cite{fan-etal-2018-controllable, liu-chen-2021-controllable, dou-etal-2021-gsum, he-etal-2022-ctrlsum, mao-etal-2022-dyle, zhang-etal-2022-summn, goyal-etal-2022-hydrasum, vig-etal-2022-exploring, bahrainian-etal-2022-newts, liu-etal-2022-length, pagnoni-etal-2023-socratic, wang-etal-2023-instructive, urlana-etal-2024-controllable .}
elements.sent-2411-175: For example, CTRLSum \cite{he-etal-2022-ctrlsum trains models by prepending a keyword sequence to the source document.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Similarly, MACSum \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum adopts prompt learning by prepending each attribute's value to the input source using a combination of hard prompts and soft prefixes.}
elements.sent-2411-177: HYDRASUM \cite{goyal-etal-2022-hydrasum leverages a single encoder and multiple decoder framework, using a mixture-of-experts where the decoders share probabilities to effectively control the attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Most controllable summarization research relied on encoder-decoder frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-179: In addition, they required attribute-specific training or custom datasets to control each attribute, limiting the flexibility of attribute manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-180: Therefore, we propose a generalizable approach to enable flexible attribute control without the need for tailored training for each attribute, leveraging underexplored LLMs for controllable summarization \cite{tang-etal-2023-context, yuan2024followinglengthconstraintsinstructions, liu-etal-2024-benchmarking .}
elements.sent-2411-181: Recently, the self-correction approach has been used to refine their initial attempts to solve complex problems \cite{weng-etal-2023-large, shinn2023reflexionlanguageagentsverbal, self-refine-2023, dhuliawala-etal-2024-chain, gou2024critic , mirroring humans.}
elements.sent-2411-182: In summarization tasks, self-correction has been employed to enhance the overall quality of summaries \cite{zhang-etal-2023-summit, sun-etal-2024-prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Unlike prior works, we focus on generating summaries tailored to user preferences, which involve a multitude of factors to consider.
elements.sent-2411-184: We conduct a systematic exploration of LLMs' controllability evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-185: While generic evaluation metrics (e.g. consistency, fluency) favor higher scores, controllability requires aligning summaries with user-specified attribute values.
elements.sent-2411-186: Previous research mainly assessed deviations from target values rather than ensuring precise control \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum .}
elements.sent-2411-187: To address this, we introduce iterative evaluation metrics, assessing whether LLMs can precisely adjust attributes, even through multiple iterations.
elements.sent-2411-188: Before evaluating a controllability, we first refine attribute measurement.
elements.sent-2411-189: We revisit attribute measurement to quantify key attributes for controllable summarization: extractiveness, length, topic, and speaker.
elements.sent-2411-190: Table \ref{tab: evaluation_survey summarizes how previous controllable summarization studies have measured each attribute.}
elements.sent-2411-191: However, the measurements for certain attributes have not yet been clearly defined.
elements.sent-2411-192: Thus, we outline our newly defined approach for attribute measurements below.
elements.sent-2411-193: Highly extractive summary is required when users need to retain the original context, such as in legal documents, whereas paraphrasing is preferred in general cases.
elements.sent-2411-194: To measure the extractiveness, MACSum \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum employs the average precision scores of ROUGE-2 and ROUGE-3.}
elements.sent-2411-195: HYDRASUM \cite{goyal-etal-2022-hydrasum calculates the proportion of words in the summary that appear in the source document as well as the average length of copied contiguous spans in the summary.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Following the definition of extractiveness, we measure this attribute as the proportion of words in the summary directly reused from the source text.
elements.figure-2411-014: 0.80\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{llcccccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Attribute} \multirow{2}{*}{Label} \multicolumn{3}{c}{\textbf{MACSum$_{Doc}$}} \multicolumn{3}{c}{\textbf{MACSum$_{Dial}$}} \cmidrule(lr){3-5} \cmidrule(lr){6-8} Distributions Relabel \# of summaries Distributions Relabel \# of summaries \multirow{3}{*}{Extractiveness} normal 35.7 - 100.0\% (85.2\%) 85.0\% 3731 53.2 - 100.0\% (86.4\%) 85.0\% 1661 high 55.0 - 100.0\% (90.0\%) 90.0\% 287 63.0 - 100.0\% (88.9\%) 90.0\% 340 fully 84.6 - 100.0\% (99.7\%) 100.0\% 260 75.9 - 100.0\% (98.4\%) 100.0\% 337 \multirow{3}{*}{Length} short 0.7 - 15.0\% (4.8\%) 7.5\% 1059 0.2 - 20.8\% (2.0\%) 7.5\% 300 normal 0.5 - 48.6\% (6.9\%) 20.0\% 2194 0.3 - 41.9\% (3.7\%) 20.0\% 1693 long 1.5 - 39.8\% (13.9\%) 32.5\% 1025 0.7 - 32.4\% (6.0\%) 32.5\% 345 Topic - 74.8 - 88.8 74.0 2013 73.6 - 87.0 74.0 2317 Speaker - - - - 75.6 - 92.0 75.0 1796 \end{tabular}} \caption{Data distributions of MACSum$_{Doc}$ and MACSum$_{Dial}$.}\label{tab: data_distribution}
elements.sent-2411-197: By controlling the length, the amount of information in the summary can be adjusted according to user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-198: Prompts suggested by earlier works specify a fixed number of sentences, e.g., "3 sentences," but this approach fails to account for variations in sentence length and does not accurately reflect the summary's actual length \cite{goyal2023news,liu-etal-2024-benchmarking, yuan2024followinglengthconstraintsinstructions .}
elements.sent-2411-199: As the summary length may vary depending on the source document, we use the length ratio instead of absolute length.
elements.sent-2411-200: Query-focused summarization (QFS), which generates summaries based on a specific query, and entity-based summarization, which focuses on a particular individual, are variations of topic-focused summarization.
elements.sent-2411-201: Similarly, most QFS methods have relied solely on ROUGE scores, evaluating generated summaries by comparing them to human-annotated references \cite{zhong-etal-2021-qmsum .}
elements.sent-2411-202: Therefore, rather than simply counting the frequency of word occurrences, we evaluate the semantic similarity between the summary and each topic-related word.
elements.sent-2411-203: We compute the embedding similarity $\mathcal{B}$ between the topic word and each word in the summary $s$: $\frac{1 {n \sum_{i \in s}\mathcal{B (topic, word_{i )$, where $n$ is the number of words in the summary.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Similar to topics, measuring the proportion of words from a specific speaker's dialogue included in the summary does not fully capture semantic elements.
elements.sent-2411-205: Therefore, we extract speaker utterances and construct speaker set $\mathcal{U}$ and leverage BERTScore F1 \cite{zhang2020bertscore to compute the embedding similarity between the summary $s$ and $\mathcal{U}$: BERTScore$(s, \mathcal{U )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Previous evaluations of the model's controllability were conducted using metrics such as mean absolute deviation (MAD) between predicted and reference summary attributes \cite{he-etal-2022-ctrlsum , or label-based control error rate (CER) and control correlation \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum .}}
elements.sent-2411-207: While these metrics capture the differences between the reference and the model-generated summaries, they do not explicitly determine whether LLMs can ultimately exert effective control over the summaries.
elements.sent-2411-208: Since precisely controlling attributes in a single generation is challenging, we evaluate LLMs' ability to iteratively refine and adjust attributes over multiple revisions.
elements.sent-2411-209: Specifically, we introduce two metrics: (1) the failure rate, which quantifies the proportion of cases where the model reaches predefined maximum iterations without achieving the desired modifications, and (2) the average iteration count required for successful attribute control.
elements.sent-2411-210: A failure or reaching the maximum number of iterations is denoted as $\circlearrowright$.
elements.sent-2411-211: We set the maximum number of iterations to 20 due to cost constraints.
elements.sent-2411-212: We set attribute-specific thresholds and iteratively regenerate summaries until they are met.
elements.sent-2411-213: Each attribute is measured using the criteria outlined in Section \ref{sec: attribute_measurement to determine its threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-214: For extractiveness and length, we consider control successful if the attribute values fall within $\pm$5 of the target value.
elements.sent-2411-215: For topic and speaker, we use the minimum embedding similarity values of the reference summaries in the training dataset (Table \ref{tab: data_distribution ) as thresholds to determine whether a summary is topic-focused or speaker-focused.}
elements.sent-2411-216: The threshold can be adjusted based on the strictness of the evaluation criteria.
elements.sent-2411-217: We use the two publicly available MACSum datasets \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum for controllable summarization.}
elements.sent-2411-218: However, existing labels are ambiguous since the criteria are not numerically defined (e.g., how short must a summary be to qualify as short?).
elements.sent-2411-219: We believe that these ambiguous criteria may confuse LLMs, so we assign clear numerical values to each label.
elements.sent-2411-220: For extractiveness, we set labels as \{normal: 85\%, high: 90\%, fully: 100\%\}, following the mean value.
elements.sent-2411-221: Unlike \citet{zhang-etal-2023-macsum , we define the summary length as a ratio of the original text rather than a fixed value.}
elements.sent-2411-222: The existing labels do not distinctly differentiate these ratios, as the average length ratios between labels show minimal differences.
elements.sent-2411-223: Specifically, short is 2.0, while normal is 3.7, indicating a relatively small gap.
elements.sent-2411-224: To allow for greater variability, we expand the range.
elements.sent-2411-225: Importantly, our method generates summaries based on the specified numerical values, regardless of predefined labels.
elements.sent-2411-226: The broader range allows for a more adaptive and effective evaluation of LLM controllability.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{fig/fig-main_com4_p.pdf} \caption{Overview of guide-to-explain system (GTE). The pink parts ({$\color{custompink}\blacksquare$}) represent the step-by-step attribute-identification, and the blue parts ({$\color{customblue}\blacksquare$}) correspond to the self-explanation guidance.} \label{fig: main}
elements.section-2411-013: Controllability of LLMs in text summarization
elements.figure-2411-016: [H] 0.7{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|c|c} Extractiveness Length Topic Phi-3-{ {medium}} 100.00\% / $\circlearrowright$ 100.00\% / $\circlearrowright$ 38.08\% / 0.22 Llama3-8B 100.00\% / $\circlearrowright$ 100.00\% / $\circlearrowright$ 57.14\% / 0.12 Llama3-70B 49.91\% / 8.05 49.36\% / 8.24 0.00\% / 0.24 GPT-3.5 49.73\% / 9.80 76.42\% / 0.00 0.00\% / 0.00 GPT-4o 39.31\% / 6.63 69.84\% / 0.00 0.38\% / 0.02 \end{tabular}} \caption{The left number represents the averaged control failure rate, and the right side denotes the average iteration count for successful control.}\label{tab: llm_naive}
elements.sent-2411-227: As research on leveraging LLMs for controllable summarization remain limited, we evaluate the controllability of various LLMs using the iterative evaluation method from Section \ref{sec: iterative_evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-228: We first provide an initial control prompt and generate a summary.
elements.sent-2411-229: If the generated summary fails to meet the specified attribute threshold, the result is fed back into the LLM’s input, prompting it to regenerate until the attribute is correctly controlled.
elements.sent-2411-230: As shown in Table \ref{tab: llm_naive , smaller-scale LLMs, such as Phi-3-{ {medium \cite{abdin2024phi and Llama3-8B \cite{dubey2024llama , partially control topic, but fail to control extractiveness and length.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-231: Similarly, large-scale LLMs such as Llama3-70B, GPT-3.5 \cite{brown2020language , and GPT-4o \cite{achiam2023gpt effectively control topic, demonstrating low failure rates.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: However, they also struggle to control extractiveness and length, showing failure rates of around 50\%.
elements.sent-2411-233: Notably, when initial attempts fail, GPT-3.5 and GPT-4o could not adjust despite multiple iterations, ultimately reaching the maximum iteration limit, resulting in an iteration count of zero.
elements.sent-2411-234: As a result, we find that generating summaries while controlling attributes remains challenging for LLMs, even with iterative attempts, especially for numerical attributes such as extractiveness and length.
elements.sent-2411-235: Therefore, we introduce a guide-to-explain (GTE) framework to adjust various attributes via LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-236: As shown in Figure \ref{fig: main , our GTE framework consists of two key phase: the step-by-step attribute identification and the self-explanation guidance.}
elements.sent-2411-237: We provide an step-by-step attribute identification to teach precise adjustment for incorrectly generated responses then guide LLMs to reflect by explaining the rationales behind their mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-238: Our approach allows the model to make appropriate adjustments in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-239: We first instruct the LLM to generate an initial summary $s'$ that reflects the specified attribute.
elements.sent-2411-240: If the LLM fails to control the attributes accurately, we provide step-by-step attribute identification (SAI) to guide the model on how to adjust the attributes.
elements.sent-2411-241: LLM may struggle to measure numerical attributes such as extractiveness or the length ratio.
elements.sent-2411-242: Therefore, we instruct the model on how to measure the attribute step-by-step so that it can revise its previously generated summary precisely.
elements.sent-2411-243: After the identification phase, we provide self-explanation guidance (SEG) to the model, guiding the model to explain why it initially failed to control the attributes.
elements.sent-2411-244: This mirrors how humans solve complex problems by reviewing their mistakes to improve future responses.
elements.sent-2411-245: Building on this, in the next iteration, the document ($d$), initial instruction ($i$), and previously generated summary ($s'$) are provided as inputs, along with SAI and SEG.
elements.sent-2411-246: Despite LLMs being known to struggle with number-related tasks \cite{akhtar-etal-2023-exploring, imani-etal-2023-mathprompter , our guidance helps the model effectively control numerical attributes by self-explaining its miscalculations before generating summaries, especially when combined with the step-by-step attribute identification phase.}
elements.sent-2411-247: We introduce GTE as a framework that integrates step-by-step attribute identification and self-explanation guidance.
elements.sent-2411-248: By receiving [$d$; $i$, $s'$; SAI; SEG] as input, the model first reflects on the reasons for the initial error before generating a revised summary.
elements.sent-2411-249: If the revised summary still fails to satisfy the attributes, we repeat the GTE process until the model generates an attribute-aligned summary.
elements.sent-2411-250: In Figure \ref{fig: main , we illustrate in detail how the GTE framework actually operates.}
elements.sent-2411-251: If the initial draft fails to properly adjust an attribute, we first provide a step-by-step guide on how to measure the attribute.
elements.sent-2411-252: Then, we guide the model to explain the miscalculation and regenerate the summary.
elements.sent-2411-253: As intended, the model mimics step-by-step attribute identification, reflects on the summary based on the identification feedback, and generates an improved summary.
elements.sent-2411-254: We evaluate the controllability of various LLMs, including Phi-3-{ {medium \cite{abdin2024phi , Llama3 series \cite{dubey2024llama , and GPT series \cite{brown2020language, achiam2023gpt .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: To analyze model performance by size, we utilize both the 8B \footnote{meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct and quantized 70B versions \footnote{casperhansen/llama-3-70b-instruct-awq of Llama3, as well as GPT-3.5 and GPT-4o.}}
elements.sent-2411-256: We use BERTScore \cite{zhang2020bertscore \footnote{https://github.com/Tiiiger/bert\_score to measure embedding similarity.}}
elements.sent-2411-257: We used two datasets for our experiments: MACSum$_{Doc}$ and the MACSum$_{Dial}$ datasets \cite{zhang-etal-2023-macsum , which comprise committee meeting transcripts and news contents, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-258: Both datasets are designed for mixed-attribute summarization that controls multiple attributes simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-259: Notably, only MACSum$_{Dial}$ has speaker attribute.
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig/fig-length_change_h3_p.pdf} \caption{The graphs show how the length changes for each iteration. The intensity of the distribution color is proportional to the number of data points, and the markers represent the average values. The red line indicates the target length, with values of 7.5, 20, and 32.5 from left to right.} \label{fig: length_change}
elements.sent-2411-260: We denote the naive iteration approach, which repeatedly adjusts attributes, as Iter.
elements.sent-2411-261: The strategy that only provides step-by-step attribute identification is defined as SAI, while our full guiding framework is referred to as GTE.
elements.sent-2411-262: As shown in Table \ref{tab: main_doc , our GTE demonstrate remarkably lower failure rates and require fewer iterations when adjusting summaries across all attributes, including challenging numerical attributes in MACSum$_{Doc}$.}
elements.sent-2411-263: Surprisingly, our method reduced the failure rate to nearly 0\% when applied to Llama3-70B and GPT-4o, successfully controlling both extractiveness and length within just 1–3 iterations.
elements.sent-2411-264: For smaller models such as Phi-3-{ {medium and Llama3-8B, which initially exhibited high failure rates, our approach significantly reduced their failure rates, demonstrating its effectiveness across different model scales.}}
elements.sent-2411-265: In particular, for long length, the most challenging attribute, our method achieved a remarkably low failure rate of just 2.32\% within an average of 3.14 iterations.
elements.sent-2411-266: Notably, length control proved to be the most challenging attribute in the MACSum$_{Dial}$ dataset.
elements.sent-2411-267: This difficulty is likely due to the original dataset being a lengthy parliamentary corpus, making it inherently challenging to generate summaries of a specific length.
elements.sent-2411-268: While the model handled short-length summaries well, the difficulty increased significantly as the requested summary length grew.
elements.sent-2411-269: In fact, for long length, both GPT-4o-Iter and GPT-4o-SAI showed a 100\% failure rate.
elements.sent-2411-270: However, our framework meaningfully improved length controllability.
elements.sent-2411-271: With GPT-4o, the average failure rate dropped below 50\%.
elements.sent-2411-272: Especially, for normal-length summaries, the failure rate further reduced from over 90\% to 9.82\%.
elements.sent-2411-273: Regarding extractiveness, the Iter and SAI of GPT-4o exhibit relatively low iteration count since the models mostly exceed the maximum iteration.
elements.sent-2411-274: While they fail nearly 80\%, our GTE demonstrates a markedly lower failure rate at 17.28\% with low iterations, demonstrating the effectiveness of our framework.
elements.sent-2411-275: To analyze how attribute changes each step, we track length adjustments per iteration (Figure \ref{fig: length_change ).}
elements.sent-2411-276: While all methods start with a similar distribution at the initial points, GTE consistently converges within approximately three iterations, maintaining a stable length adjustment pattern across samples.
elements.sent-2411-277: In contrast, Iter and SAI show inconsistent changes across samples, leading higher variance in length changes.
elements.sent-2411-278: This demonstrates that our method enables robust attribute control with fewer iterations, regardless of the data sample.
elements.sent-2411-279: For the experiment, we utilize Llama3-70B and randomly selected 110 samples from the MACSum$_{Doc}$ test set.
elements.sent-2411-280: We observe that LLMs control linguistic attributes (topic and speaker) better than numerical attributes (extractiveness and length).
elements.sent-2411-281: This aligns with previous research in mathematical reasoning, where LLM struggle with numerical features \cite{akhtar-etal-2023-exploring , highlighting a broader challenge in precisely handling numerical constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-282: From the perspective of the summarization task, extractiveness and length control the structure of the summary, whereas topic and speaker control its contents.
elements.sent-2411-283: Our findings suggest that LLMs are proficient at adjusting content to align with user preferences, but they struggle to generate summaries in a desired structural format.
elements.figure-2411-020: 0.7{ \begin{tabular}{l|ccccc|c|c|c} \multirow{2}{*}{Model} \multicolumn{5}{c|}{UniEval} \multirow{2}{*}{QuestEval} \multirow{2}{*}{BERTScore} \multirow{2}{*}{ROUGE-1} \cmidrule(lr){2-6} Coherence Consistency Fluency Relevance Overall Iter (Ext) 0.820 0.800 0.859 0.696 0.794 0.523 0.826 0.194 SAI (Ext) 0.884 0.843 0.905 0.785 0.864 0.554 0.848 0.229 Iter (Len) 0.836 0.803 0.836 0.759 0.808 0.484 0.829 0.235 SAI (Len) 0.934 0.834 0.942 0.887 0.899 0.548 0.867 0.270 GTE (Ext) 0.941 0.873 0.937 0.880 0.908 0.590 0.861 0.236 GTE (Len) 0.937 0.840 0.944 0.901 0.905 0.553 0.868 0.272 \end{tabular} }\caption{Quality of the controlled summaries.}\label{tab: quality}
elements.sent-2411-284: We evaluate the quality of summaries generated by GTE.
elements.sent-2411-285: We mainly use UniEval \cite{zhong-etal-2022-towards and QuestEval \cite{scialom-etal-2021-questeval , as they highly correlate with human judgement and assess the overall quality of the summary itself.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: UniEval is a multi-dimensional evaluator that assesses coherence, consistency, fluency, and relevance of summaries.
elements.sent-2411-287: QuestEval measures precision and recall by leveraging question answering framework to compare the content between the source document and the generated summary without relying on the reference summary.
elements.sent-2411-288: Table \ref{tab: quality shows that our method's summaries outperform across all UniEval dimensions and QuestEval, demonstrating effective attribute control while maintaining overall summary quality.}
elements.sent-2411-289: Relevance assesses how well a summary retains key information compared to the reference.
elements.sent-2411-290: While Iter and SAI generate misaligned summaries with lower relevance scores, GTE effectively aligns them, leading to a substantial gain.
elements.sent-2411-291: Although previous studies have shown that ROUGE scores \cite{lin-2004-rouge are insufficient for assessing summary quality \cite{scialom-etal-2021-questeval, zhong-etal-2022-towards, ryu-etal-2024-multi , and given that our goal is to control the summary rather than make it similar to the reference, we still include ROUGE and BERTScore \cite{zhang2020bertscore in our evaluation to provide clearer assessment.}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: Our framework exhibits higher scores than other approaches, demonstrating through various evaluation metrics that GTE not only enhances controllability, but also improves overall summary quality.
elements.sent-2411-293: We extend our evaluation to assess whether LLMs could precisely handle mixed-attribute control.
elements.sent-2411-294: While models manage to control linguistic attributes but struggle with numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-295: Simultaneous control over all attributes remains challenging for all iterative methods, including GTE.
elements.sent-2411-296: Our GTE framework guides LLMs to identify the causes of their errors and regenerate summaries by incorporating this feedback.
elements.sent-2411-297: However, in a mixed attribute setting, the model needs to handle multiple SAI and GS for each attribute simultaneously, increasing the cognitive load and making precise control of all attributes more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-298: Therefore, unlike single-attribute evaluation, which assesses whether attributes are precisely controlled, we evaluate mixed-attribute control by measuring errors using root mean squared error (rMSE).
elements.sent-2411-299: We compare the error between the attributes of the generated summary and the requested values, providing a more flexible evaluation of attribute control.
elements.sent-2411-300: Discovering the challenges in precisely controlling all attributes simultaneously, we introduce a sequential planning strategy, min-planning, which gradually adjusts attributes from the ill-controlled with the initial draft using GTE.
elements.sent-2411-301: Figure \ref{fig: mixed shows the results comparing single attribute control with iterations to mixed-attribute control using min-planning on the MACSum$_{Doc}$ dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-302: We refer to the initial summary in the mixed-attribute control as the mixed-draft.
elements.sent-2411-303: The min-planning method shows a modest improvement in controlling both attributes compared to the mixed-draft.
elements.sent-2411-304: Attributes are still not fully controlled compared to single-attribute models, highlighting the challenge of balancing multiple attributes.
elements.sent-2411-305: We anticipate that modifying one attribute often disrupts previously adjusted attributes due to correlations.
elements.sent-2411-306: For example, even if length is adjusted first, it may still change when extractiveness is controlled.
elements.sent-2411-307: Additionally, min-planning only adjusts each attribute once without iterations, which may account for its failure to fully control the attributes.
elements.sent-2411-308: A single refinement is often insufficient to control all attributes, whereas GTE iteratively regenerates the summary until the attribute is successfully adjusted in single-attribute control.
elements.sent-2411-309: Exploring ways for LLMs to control multiple attributes simultaneously would be promising future work.
elements.sent-2411-310: In this work, we systematically explore the controllability of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-311: To this end, we revisit the measurement of various attributes.
elements.sent-2411-312: We evaluate the controllability of LLMs via iterative assessment, finding that LLMs struggle more with numeric attribute control than linguistic attributes.
elements.sent-2411-313: To address this, we propose a guide-to-explain (GTE) approach, where the model is guided to explain its misalignments through attribute identification and then grounds this explanation to generate better-controlled summaries in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-314: Furthermore, we demonstrate the high quality of controlled summaries using various generic summarization evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-315: We explore the controllability of various attributes in LLMs and introduced a novel guide-to-explain (GTE) framework to address challenges in numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-316: While GTE enhanced successfully control over challenging numerical attributes, it still struggled with highly correlated mixed numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-317: Additionally, min-planning, which adjusts attributes in order of least alignment, also faced difficulties achieving precise control.
